retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Schistocytes; Schistocytes are disrupted red blood cells. Helmet cells are a subset where the red cell has been "chopped" mechanically, leaving behind a roughly semicircular residual. It looks akin to a helmet with a straight border next to the semicircle and sharp, angular edges. Triangular cells are the remnants, the triangle-shaped detritus of red cells; they are very small fragments with significant distortions from significant disruptions. Schistocytes might actually be 'normal' in adults and full-term neonates if their presence is < 1% of the population. [13] In newborns, one may find pyknocytes, fragile and contracted erythrocytes. They can persist in premature babies for up to the first six months of life. Sometimes schistocytes can be difficult to identify if they are embedded in a clouded field of other cell types like echinocytes, acanthocytes, and created (shriveled) red blood cells. In this situation, noting an increase in the LDH, the RDW (measurement of anisocytosis), or the presence of polychromasia may offer a measure of help in making the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: When examining a peripheral blood smear under a microscope, specific findings can provide valuable insights into the underlying condition. Observations related to certain types of anemia are as follows: Microangiopathic hemolysis : In conditions characterized by microangiopathic hemolysis, such as TTP, HUS, and DIC, a peripheral blood smear examination may reveal several abnormal RBC shapes. These abnormalities include helmet cells, fragmented RBCs (schistocytes), and other RBC fragments. Autoimmune hemolytic anemias or hereditary spherocytosis : Spherocytes (small, round RBCs lacking central pallor) may be present in some cases.

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Schistocytes or helmet cells present on a peripheral blood smear are diagnostic of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Hepatic histology shows fatty infiltration, intravascular fibrin deposits, neutrophilic infiltrate, sinusoidal obstruction, intrahepatic vascular congestion, hepatic necrosis, and periportal hemorrhage. This may eventually result in intraparenchymal or subcapsular hemorrhage and capsular rupture. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In HTRs, a peripheral blood smear will often reveal evidence of hemolysis, which may include the presence of keratocytes, helmet cells, bite cells, blister cells, spherocytes, or microspherocytes. These abnormal RBC shapes and forms are indicative of the destruction of RBCs that can occur in such reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: A bite cell has a semi-circular indentation as if someone took a bite from it. Echinocytes are erythrocytes with uniform projections all equidistant apart. Schistocyte is a term that refers to an RBC fragment that has been sheared. A sickle cell is an RBC with a crescentic shape. Spherocytes are erythrocytes that have no central pallor and are uniformly red throughout. Stomatocytes have a linear central clearing rather than a circular one. Target cells have a red center with a central clearing. Teardrop cells taper on one end. One last group of erythrocyte descriptors evaluated with light microscopy is inclusion bodies. These are cytoplasmic or nuclear findings that give clinical clues. For example, Heinz bodies are small round masses seen with supravital or Heinz stains that indicate hemolysis. Howell-Jolly bodies are solid, large, round masses found in the hemoglobin of the cell, are visualized with Wright-stain, and have many clinical significances. Pappenheimer bodies are multiple, small blue inclusion bodies throughout the entire RBC, indicating iron overload, hyposplenism, or myelodysplasia. Finally, basophilic stippling is blue/purple granules seen in thalassemia, lead poisoning, and other pathologies. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cells (drepanocytes): Seen in sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Poikilocytosis is the result of internal damage or external (environmental) trauma. Poikilocytosis causes, generally speaking, it can be inherited or acquired. Inherited conditions can be caused by genetic abnormalities or mutations. Acquired conditions usually develop later in life. The following list provides a general overlay based on etiology. Inherited causes of poikilocytosis include: In sickle cell anemia, RBCs are an abnormal crescent, an elongated spiculated shape known as sickle cells or drepanocytes [1] Thalassemia, a genetic disorder in which abnormal hemoglobin is made, contains target cells or codocytes Hereditary spherocytosis contains spherocytes [2] Pyruvate kinase deficiency contains echinocytes or burr cells Hereditary elliptocytosis contains elliptocytes [3] McLeod syndrome is a rare genetic disorder that contains acanthocytes Acquired causes of poikilocytosis include:

retrieved_docs.title: Normochromic Normocytic Anemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Decreased RBC Survival and Bone Marrow Infiltration: Several studies have identified a mild shortening of the red-cell lifespan in normocytic normochromic anemia cases, which appears to be due to an extracorpuscular factor or intrinsic abnormality of the red cells. The RBC survival is usually not markedly shortened, and marrow function should compensate for the reduced survival. In hemolytic anemias, the etiology of premature erythrocyte destruction is diverse and can be due to conditions such as intrinsic membrane defects, abnormal hemoglobin, erythrocyte enzymatic defects, immune destruction of erythrocytes, mechanical injury, and hypersplenism. Hemolysis may also be intramedullary, occurring when fragile red blood cell (RBC) precursors are destroyed in the bone marrow before they are released into circulation. [15] Bone marrow changes also lead to physical obstruction and destruction of the bone marrow microenvironment.

retrieved_docs.title: Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The peripheral smear will show small-sized RBCs with pencil cells. The microcytic cells will have a large zone of central pallor and a small peripheral rim of hemoglobin.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: SAO, also called stomatocytosis elliptocytosis, is predominantly observed in regions endemic to malaria. This condition is characterized by mild or no hemolysis and provides resistance against Plasmodium falciparum infection. The peripheral blood smear of individuals with SAO exhibits stomatocytes, ovalocytes, and macro-ovalocytes. The underlying cause of this entity is a deletion within the SLC441 gene that encodes erythrocyte band 3. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Common hereditary elliptocytosis represents the most prevalent form of hereditary elliptocytosis, and patients are typically asymptomatic. Neonates with this condition may experience transient hemolysis, which typically resolves within the first year of life. In cases of severe hemolytic anemia and jaundice in neonates, transfusion and phototherapy may be necessary. The defining characteristic of common hereditary elliptocytosis is the presence of elliptical-shaped RBCs in the peripheral blood smear, constituting anywhere from 15% to 100% of the total RBCs. The peripheral blood smear may also observe spherocytes, stomatocytes, and poikilocytes (fragmented cells).

retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Clinicopathologic Correlations -- Basophilic Stippling
retrieved_docs.content: Basophilic stippling is seen in various conditions, such as lead and other heavy metal poisonings, infections, liver disease, thalassemia, and some cases of megaloblastic anemia. Basophilic stippling in microcytic, normochromic anemia highly suggests a thalassemia trait.

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dacrocytes (teardrop cells): Seen in beta-thalassemia, leukemia, hemolytic anemia, myelofibrosis, and megaloblastic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Anemia -- History and Physical -- Clinical History
retrieved_docs.content: In situations where the patient cannot communicate, obtaining as much information as possible from emergency medical services (EMS) personnel or individuals at the bedside is crucial. Additionally, reviewing previous medical charts, if available, can offer valuable insights into the patient's medical history and aid in understanding the underlying cause of anemia. Note any medical alert devices (ie, necklaces).

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A peripheral blood smear examination typically follows the MCV findings. This smear helps determine the size of erythrocytes relative to leukocytes. Microcytes are smaller and often exhibit a larger central area of pallor compared to normal erythrocytes, especially in cases of iron-deficiency anemia or anemia of chronic disease. Depending on the underlying cause of microcytosis, microcytic erythrocytes may also display features such as target cells and anisopoikilocytosis in thalassemia or basophilic stippling, characteristic of lead poisoning. Sideroblasts, on the other hand, are observed exclusively in bone marrow aspirates.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: HPP stands out as the most severe form of hereditary elliptocytosis, predominantly affecting African-American neonates. These infants typically present with neonatal jaundice and persistent hemolytic anemia throughout their lives. The peripheral blood smear in HPP reveals poikilocytes and spherocytes with rare elliptocytes. Microspherocytes are present, with a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 30 to 50 gL. In some cases, the cellular morphology may resemble those of thermal burn patients, displaying microspherocytes and erythrocyte fragments. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with قساح. A principal manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (low blood oxygen), which is also mapped to الاِختِنَاق, indicating a link between hypoxemia and asphyxic events. Recognizing that patients with sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) may present with قساح and experience نقص الأكسجين في الدم that can progress to or be interpreted as الاِختِنَاق is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and timely therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hematologic disorder that can clinically manifest as adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest. This respiratory complication is classified under the broader category of respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which themselves are subtypes of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). The disease’s manifestation of low blood‑oxygen levels (نقص الأكسجين في الدم) reflects the same pathophysiologic process that defines asphyxia. Consequently, sickle cell anemia is linked to hypoxemia, which underlies the development of respiratory failure, situating the condition within a hierarchical framework that progresses from the specific hematologic disease to respiratory disorder and ultimately to the general concept of asphyxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia). The condition الاِختِنَاق (apnea) is also mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, linking apnea to the same hypoxemic state seen in sickle cell disease. Together, these relationships underscore that patients with داء الكريات المنجلية are at risk for severe respiratory complications, including hypoxemia and apnea, and highlight the importance of vigilant respiratory monitoring and oxygen management in their care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2612
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is classified as a hemoglobinopathy. In the context of this disease, respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is identified as a clinical complication, and the Arabic term الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia) is likewise listed as an allowed qualifier complication. Both respiratory failure and asphyxia are closely linked to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), which is a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The mapping of الاِختِنَاق to نقص الأكسجين في الدم highlights that low blood‑oxygen levels are a central pathophysiological feature that underlies these serious complications. Recognizing that sickle cell disease can precipitate severe respiratory compromise and hypoxemic states is essential for effective patient monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2254
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory complications, including respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. In addition, داء الكريات المنجلية is linked to فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia). These associations highlight that patients with sickle cell disease may experience both critical respiratory events and lipid metabolism disturbances, underscoring the need for comprehensive monitoring and management of respiratory function and cholesterol levels in this population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: that warrant careful ophthalmologic monitoring in patients with thalassemic disorders. Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2537
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية (sickle cell disease). داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest, and it manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia). Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is recognized as a complication of داء الكريات المنجلية, as are اضطراب المريء (esophageal disorder) and الاِختِنَاق (choking). The complication الاِختِنَاق is mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, underscoring that choking can lead to or reflect hypoxemia. Together, these relationships highlight that respiratory compromise, hypoxemia, and related complications such as esophageal dysfunction and choking are central features of sickle cell disease, informing clinical monitoring and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as dاء الكريات المنجلية (sickle cell disease). Dاء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest, and it is also clinically associated with قساح. Radiotherapy is an allowed qualifier for both dاء الكريات المنجلية and respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult), and the use of radiotherapy can be qualified by قساح. These connections indicate that radiotherapy may be considered in the therapeutic context of sickle cell disease and adult respiratory failure, and that the presence of قساح influences how radiotherapy is qualified or applied.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) is clinically associated with hypocalcemia (نقص كالسيوم الدم), indicating that patients with this immune‑mediated destruction of red blood cells may also exhibit low blood calcium levels. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it suggests that calcium monitoring and appropriate management of hypocalcemia should be considered when evaluating and treating individuals with autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 555
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) is classified as a distinct hematologic disorder and is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest, as well as with قساح. It commonly manifests as hypoxemia (نقص الأكسجين في الدم). Adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest (respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult)) is a subtype of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which itself is a subtype of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). Asphyxia is mapped to hypoxemia, underscoring the central role of reduced blood oxygen levels in these conditions. Radiotherapy (radioterapie) is an allowed qualifier for sickle cell anemia, for adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest, and for asphyxia, and it can be further qualified by قساح. This interconnected framework highlights how sickle cell disease, severe respiratory complications, and hypoxemia are related, and it delineates the contexts in which radiotherapy may be considered as a therapeutic qualifier.
